description: Government-funded employment support, training, apprenticeships, or welfare-to-work programme. Evaluated using Magenta Book 3Es framework.

cost_per_participant.note: Light-touch job brokerage at the low end, intensive supported employment at the high end

cost_per_job_outcome.note: Sustained = 6+ months in employment. Includes deadweight.

typical_benefits.description: JSA/UC savings when participants move into employment
typical_benefits.monetisation: UC standard allowance (~GBP 4,700/year single, ~GBP 7,400/year couple) + housing element. Or use unit-costs-outcomes.json fiscal cost of unemployment.

typical_benefits.description: Income tax and NICs from newly employed participants
typical_benefits.monetisation: Use tax-parameters.json effective rates by earnings level

typical_benefits.description: Participant income net of benefit withdrawal
typical_benefits.monetisation: Earnings minus benefit taper. Wider social value per Green Book.

typical_benefits.description: Employment reduces mental health service use, GP visits
typical_benefits.monetisation: Unit costs from GMCA database (mental health episode ~GBP 1,750)

typical_benefits.description: Employment is associated with reduced offending
typical_benefits.monetisation: Use crime unit costs with caution (attribution is weak)
Lifetime earnings uplift from qualifications (Level 2: ~GBP 63k, Level 3: ~GBP 94k)

default_parameters.appraisal_period_note: Employment programmes typically evaluated over 5-10 years. Benefit persistence depends on job sustainability and wage progression.

optimism_bias_pct.note: Not standard for programme CBA. Use sensitivity analysis instead.

deadweight.note: Employment programmes have relatively high deadweight: many participants would have found work eventually. DWP evaluation evidence suggests 30-50%.

displacement.note: Job displacement is moderate for employment programmes. Higher for retail/service sector placements.

leakage.note: Some participants may take jobs outside the target area.

default_parameters.benefit_decay_note: Assume 10% annual decay in employment persistence (some participants return to unemployment). This is conservative; DWP evidence suggests 5-15% annual exit rates from sustained employment.

reference_bcr_range.note: Employment programmes are hard to monetise fully. Fiscal BCR (Exchequer savings only) is typically lower than social BCR. High BCRs from intensive programmes targeting long-term unemployed with multiple barriers.
reference_cases: Work Programme (2011-2017): fiscal BCR ~0.8-1.2 depending on cohort (DWP evaluation 2018)
Future Jobs Fund (2009-2011): BCR 2.0 (DWP/CESI evaluation, high due to direct job creation)
Troubled Families Programme (2012-2022): fiscal BCR 1.5-2.5 (MHCLG evaluation)
What Works Centre: employment programmes achieving SMS Level 3+ typically show 10-20% employment rate uplift over control

typical_risks: High deadweight: many participants would have found employment anyway (especially in strong labour markets)
Creaming: providers may select participants most likely to succeed, inflating apparent impact
Parking: hard-to-help participants may receive minimal support
Job sustainability: initial employment may not persist (measure at 6, 12, and 24 months)
Displacement: programme participants may take jobs from non-participants
Economic cycle: programme effectiveness is heavily influenced by the macro labour market

evidence_base.what_works: What Works Centre for Local Economic Growth: Employment Training Evidence Review (2016). Overall evidence strength: moderate. Key finding: intensive, personalised support with employer engagement is most effective.
evidence_base.sms_levels_achieved: Most UK employment programme evaluations are SMS Level 2 (before-and-after with controls). A few achieve Level 3 (DID with comparison areas). The Work Programme RCT (SMS Level 5) is the exception.
evidence_base.common_evaluation_gap: Many programmes lack baseline data. Recommendation: collect participant data at registration, not just at programme exit.

publications: What Works Centre for Local Economic Growth: Employment Training (2016)
